revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Hirasaki Shrine]][[fr:Hirakiki-jinja]][[ja:枚聞神社]][[zh:枚聞神社]] {{Short description|Japanese Shinto shrine in Ibusuki, Kagoshima, Kyushu}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hirasaki-jinja
枚聞神社 | image = File:Hirakiki shrine.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'', or main shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Kagoshima Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|31.20777778|N|130.53972222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Mount Kaimon|qid=Q713847|1=Mount Kaimon}} | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 1366, Kaimonjutcho, Ibusuki-shi, Kagoshima-ken 891-0603 | website = {{URL|http://www.ichinomiya.gr.jp/095.html}} | architecture_style ={{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} | festival = October 14 to 16 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kaimon Jutchō|qid=Q17226312|1=Kaimon Jutchō}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hirasaki Shrine'''|枚聞神社|Hirasaki-jinja}}, also known as '''Hirakiki-jinja''' is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Kaimonjuttcho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Ibusuki, Kagoshima| Ibusuki]] in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Along with the [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]], it claims the position of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Satsuma Province|en|Satsuma Province|qid=Q667475|lt=Satsuma Province|1=Satsuma Province}}. The main festival of the shrine is held annually from October 14 to 16.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Hirasaki Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ōhirumenomuchi-no-Mikoto'''| 大日孁貴命|}}, who is identified as [[Amaterasu]] * {{nihongo|'''Go-o San-ji shin'''|五男三女神 |}}, five male and three creator ''kami'' listed in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' ==History== The origins of Hirasaka Jinja are unknown. According to the shrine's legend, it was founded in 708 AD, and appears to have originally been based on a mountain cult centered on {{ill|Mount Kaimon|en|Mount Kaimon|qid=Q713847|lt=Mount Kaimon|1=Mount Kaimon}}. It is said that it was originally located at the southern foot of Mount Kaimon, but due to an eruption during the {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} era (859 - 876), it was moved to its current location at the northern foot of the mountain. The first reliable historical document to mention the shrine is an entry in the "[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]" dated March 20, 860, and in later entries dated April 7, 866 and on October 9, 882. The shrine also appears In the [[Engishiki|''Engishiki Jinmyōchō'']] (Divination List of Shrines) of 927, where it is listed as "Hiyaki Jinja". There was considerable variation throughout historical records as to the actual ''kami'' venerated at this shrine, with most {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} records stating that it was {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}} or [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] and early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} records stating that it was variously [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], {{ill|Toyotama-hime|en|Toyotama-hime|qid=Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|1=Toyotama-hime}}, or [[Konohanasakuya-hime]] in addition to the current [[Amaterasu]]. Since its location facing the open sea, the shrine has long been worshiped for protection of navigation and in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, it attracted the reverence of envoys from the {{ill|Ryukyu Islands|en|Ryukyu Islands|qid=Q6897416|lt=Ryukyu Islands|1=Ryukyu Islands}}.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, Hirasaki Shrine was under the protection of the Ei clan, a powerful vassal of the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|qid=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu clan|1=Shimazu clan}}, but in 1571, the shrine was largely destroyed in an internal conflict between branches of the clan. However, it was soon restored under the protection of the Shimazu clan, who decided their strategy by drawing lots at the shrine. The current shrine building was donated by [[Shimazu Yoshihiro]] in 1610 and remodeled by [[Shimazu Shigehide]] in 1787. Since the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine has been in a fierce rivalry with [[Nitta Shrine (Satsumasendai City)|Nitta Shrine]] (in [[:en:Satsumasendai|Satsumasendai]]) over the position of ''ichinomiya'' of Satsuma Province. Nitta Shrine, which was also one of the five branch shrines of [[Usa Jingū]], gradually gained the upper hand, and during the Meiji period era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, Nitta Shrine was rated as a {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank |国幣中社|Kokuhei Chusha}}, whereas Hirasaki Shrine was accorded the lower Rank of {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|Kokuhei Shosha}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located 800 meters north of {{ill|Kaimon Station|en|Kaimon Station|qid=Q6348036|lt=Kaimon Station|1=Kaimon Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|qid=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[ Ibusuki Makurazaki Line ]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Hirakiki-jinja}} *[http://www.ichinomiya.gr.jp/095.html Official Website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[en:Hirasaki Shrine]] {{Commons category|Hirakiki-jinja}} [[fr:Hirakiki-jinja]] [[ja:枚聞神社]] [[zh:枚聞神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3136198}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
